answer:
The **comitatus**—the lord-retainer bond—is a central heroic ideal in Old English elegiac poetry, depicting a reciprocal relationship between a lord (often termed *gold-gifa*, *ring-gifa*, *dryhten*, or *goldwine*) and his retainers (*þegn*, *beorna*, or *hearpera* band). The lord provides treasure (gold, rings, land, mead, feasts) in exchange for loyalty, military service, wise counsel, and praise through song; retainers, in turn, offer unwavering devotion, often enduring exile (*wræclastas*) and sorrow (*geomor*) upon the lord's death or loss.[^1] This bond structures hall-life (*heall-drincung*, *medu-drincung*), where generosity elevates fame (*dom*) amid *wyrd*'s decay.[^2]

It recurs as a motif of lost stability across the elegies:

- **The Wanderer** (ch. 11): The exiled speaker mourns his dead "gold-friend" (lord), recalling youthful welcome at the "gift-throne" (*giefstol*) amid hall-warriors and treasure-taking; he seeks a new "treasure-giver" (*beorna brytta*) for comfort, but finds only sorrow without protectors.[^3] A wise retainer binds grief inwardly until remedy appears.[^4]

- **Maxims I** (ch. 6): Codifies norms—a queen dispenses horses/treasures at "mead-giving" (*medu-drincunge*) before retainers (*þegnas*), greeting the "protector of princes" (*cyningas weard*) first; lords on the "gift-throne" (*giefstole*) share gold justly.[^5]

- **Widsith** (ch. 14): The scop boasts receiving "memorable treasure" (*meara wæte*) in halls from lords like Eormanric; generous rulers reward song before "veterans" (*hearpera*) to raise renown.[^6]

- **Deor** (ch. 13): The displaced scop laments losing his "loyal lord" (*dryhtne leof*) and "land-right" (*londriht*) granted by the "protector of earls" (*eorla hleofre*), enduring *wyrd*'s turn.[^7]

- **The Ruin** (ch. 16): Evokes vanished mead-halls (*meduhæle*) "full of human joys," where gold-bright warriors (*winemagum*) once gazed on treasure amid a "great host's clamor."[^8]

- **The Seafarer** (ch. 5): Even the proudest with a "dear lord" (*dryhten leof*) forgoes harp/rings for sea-longing, underscoring the bond's fragility.[^9]

The elegies frame this ideal against transience: halls crumble, lords die, retainers wander, urging Christian *lufu* (divine grace) as true stability.[^10]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [¶1-2](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9cca5e).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6, [¶1](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [¶2](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [¶3](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-5bdc65).

[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6, [¶1](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).

[^6]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, [¶1,10](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-2f6bd3).

[^7]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 13, [¶7](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-6b7e51).

[^8]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 16, [¶2](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16-wraetlic-is-thes-wealstan-wyrde-yebraecon-burgsted/translation/#p-ba51da).

[^9]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 5, [¶4](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-549618).

[^10]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [¶3](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-5bdc65).
